title: Salmonellosis
authors: David A. Pegues, Samuel I. Miller

items.title: Understanding Salmonella Serotypes and Their Host Specificity
items.content: Salmonella species are divided into two main types: S. enterica, which includes serotypes pathogenic to humans, and S. bongori. The former is further subdivided into six subspecies, with the majority of human-pathogenic serotypes found in subspecies I. Serotyping is crucial for identifying Salmonella strains based on surface antigens such as O, Vi, and H.
keyPoints: S. enterica contains almost all serotypes pathogenic to humans.
Serotyping involves antigenically diverse structures: O antigen (lipopolysaccharide), Vi antigen (restricted to S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi C), and flagellar H antigen.
~2600 serovars exist, named often after the city of identification.

items.title: Laboratory Identification and Epidemiological Investigation of Salmonella
items.content: The initial identification of Salmonellae in clinical labs relies on growth characteristics such as acid production from glucose fermentation, nitrate reduction, and absence of cytochrome oxidase. Most laboratories use agglutination reactions for serogrouping, which covers ~99% of human infections. Whole-genome sequencing aids in tracing foodborne outbreaks and multidrug-resistant strains.
keyPoints: Salmonellae produce acid on glucose fermentation, reduce nitrates, do not produce cytochrome oxidase.
Motility by peritrichous flagella and H2S production are key characteristics except for S. Gallinarum-Pullorum (non-motile) and S. Typhi (no H2S).
~1% of clinical isolates ferment lactose, requiring high suspicion for detection.
Agglutination reactions define O-antigen serogroups A, B, C1, C2, D, E covering ~99% of human infections.
Whole-genome sequencing is used in epidemiologic investigations.

items.title: Ingestion and Susceptibility Factors in Salmonella Infections
items.content: Salmonella infections begin with ingestion of contaminated food or water, with an infectious dose ranging from 200 to $10^{6}$ CFU. Conditions that reduce stomach acidity or intestinal integrity increase susceptibility.
keyPoints: Infectious dose: 200 to $10^{6}$ CFU
Increased risk factors: age <1 year, acid suppression therapy, achlorhydric disease, inflammatory bowel disease, cytotoxic chemotherapy, prior gastrointestinal surgery, antibiotic administration

items.title: Mechanism of Salmonella Entry and Survival in Host Cells
items.content: S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi penetrate the gut lining via M cells and induce endocytosis through type III secretion system, altering host cell cytoskeleton. They survive in macrophages by modifying their outer membrane and secreting proteins to remodel phagosomes.
keyPoints: Entry mechanism: penetration of mucus layer, bacterium-mediated endocytosis
Survival strategy: alteration of outer membrane via PhoP/PhoQ system, type III secretion system for macrophage cytoplasmic delivery

items.title: Pathophysiology and Immune Response in Typhoidal vs. Nontyphoidal Salmonella Infections
items.content: Typhoidal salmonellae disseminate via macrophages, causing systemic symptoms and hepatosplenomegaly due to immune response. NTS gastroenteritis involves neutrophil infiltration and interleukin 8 induction, leading to inflammatory diarrhea.
keyPoints: Typhoidal infection: dissemination in macrophages, cytokine secretion, hepatosplenomegaly
NTS gastroenteritis: polymorphonuclear leukocyte infiltration, interleukin 8 induction, tetrathionate utilization

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Enteric Fever
items.content: Enteric fever, caused by S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi, is primarily transmitted through fecal contamination of food or water. The disease's prevalence varies significantly between developed and endemic regions.
keyPoints: S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi have no animal reservoirs; humans are the only hosts.
Annual global cases: 9.2–21 million typhoid, 5 million paratyphoid, with 110,000–280,000 deaths.
Highest incidence rates (>1000 per 100,000 children) in urban areas of the Indian subcontinent and certain African regions.
Risk factors include contaminated water/food, poor sanitation, and prior H. pylori infection.

items.title: Antibiotic Resistance in Enteric Fever
items.content: The emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) and extensively drug-resistant (XDR) strains of S. Typhi has complicated treatment, particularly in regions like Southeast Asia.
keyPoints: MDR strains emerged in the 1980s with resistance to chloramphenicol, ampicillin, and trimethoprim.
DSC strains developed due to fluoroquinolone use, characterized by MIC ≥0.125 μg/mL for ciprofloxacin.
XDR S. Typhi H58 clone emerged in 2016 in Pakistan with ESBL resistance.
Azithromycin resistance has been noted where it is used as a first-line treatment.

items.title: Enteric Fever Epidemiology in the United States
items.content: In the U.S., enteric fever cases are primarily travel-associated, with a significant number linked to regions like India and Pakistan.
keyPoints: CDC estimates 5700 typhoid cases annually, far exceeding reported cases (~350).
Median age of patients: 23 years for typhoid, 29 for paratyphi.
78% of enteric fever cases linked to international travel, mainly to the Indian subcontinent.
66% of U.S. S. Typhi strains are DSC; ~10% resistant to ampicillin, chloramphenicol, and TMP-SMX.

items.title: Importance of High Index of Suspicion for Enteric Fever
items.content: Enteric fever presents with variable symptoms; therefore, a high index of suspicion is crucial when evaluating patients with fever and recent travel history to developing countries.
keyPoints: Fever documented in >75% at presentation.
Abdominal pain reported in only 30–40%.
High suspicion needed for potentially fatal systemic illness.

items.title: Complications and Timing of Typhoid Fever
items.content: Typhoid fever complications are significant, often occurring in ~27% of hospitalized patients, with gastrointestinal issues like bleeding and perforation typically arising in the third and fourth weeks.
keyPoints: Complications occur in ~27% of hospitalized patients.
Gastrointestinal bleeding (6%) and intestinal perforation (1%).
Most common in the third and fourth weeks.

items.title: Chronic Carriage and Associated Risks
items.content: Up to 10% of untreated typhoid fever patients become chronic carriers, with an increased risk of gallbladder cancer due to biofilm formation on gallstones.
keyPoints: Up to 10% develop mild relapse post-treatment.
2–5% become chronic asymptomatic carriers for >1 year.
Increased risk of gallbladder cancer associated with chronic carriage.

items.title: Consider Enteric Fever in Febrile Travelers from High-Risk Regions
items.content: In febrile travelers returning from developing regions, particularly the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asian or African areas, enteric fever should be considered. The clinical presentation is non-specific, necessitating differential diagnoses including malaria, viral hepatitis, bacterial enteritis, dengue fever, rickettsial infections, leptospirosis, amebic liver abscesses, and acute HIV infection.
keyPoints: No specific laboratory test other than culture is diagnostic for enteric fever.
Leukopenia and neutropenia are detectable in 15–25% of cases; leukocytosis more common among children, during the first 10 days, or with complications like intestinal perforation.
Liver function tests and muscle enzyme levels may be moderately elevated.

items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for Enteric Fever
items.content: The definitive diagnosis of enteric fever requires isolation of S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi from blood, bone marrow, other sterile sites, rose spots, stool, or intestinal secretions. Blood culture sensitivity is ~40–60%, reduced by prior antimicrobial use or early illness phase. Bone marrow culture has ~80% sensitivity and is less affected by antibiotics. Stool cultures are more likely positive in the third week of untreated infection.
keyPoints: Blood culture sensitivity: ~40–60%; lower with small sample volume, prior antibiotic use, or first week of illness.
Bone marrow culture sensitivity: ~80%, not reduced by up to 5 days of prior antibiotics.
Stool cultures negative in 60–70% during the first week; positivity increases in untreated patients by third week.
Rapid immunodiagnostic tests (e.g., Tubex, Typhidot) have sensitivities ~70–80%, specificities ~80–90%; not sufficiently accurate to replace blood cultures.

items.title: Antibiotic Choice Based on Resistance Patterns
items.content: The choice of antibiotics for treating enteric fever depends significantly on the susceptibility patterns of S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi strains in specific regions, especially due to varying resistance levels.
keyPoints: Case-fatality rate is 2.5% overall but rises to 4.5% among hospitalized patients and 10–30% if untreated.
Antibiotic therapy reduces mortality to <1% when administered promptly.
Ceftriaxone, fluoroquinolones, or azithromycin are generally effective; however, resistance patterns necessitate specific choices.
Fluoroquinolones should not be used empirically in regions with high ciprofloxacin-resistant strains (e.g., Indian subcontinent and some African locales).
Ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, and oral cefixime are effective against MDR enteric fever.
Azithromycin is recommended for treating uncomplicated enteric fever with resistant strains.

items.title: Management of Complicated Enteric Fever
items.content: Patients presenting with complications such as persistent vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal distension require hospitalization and parenteral antibiotics based on susceptibility profiles.
keyPoints: Hospitalize patients with severe symptoms like persistent vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal distension.
Administer supportive therapy along with a third-generation cephalosporin, fluoroquinolone, or carbapenem depending on resistance patterns.
Continue treatment for at least 10 days or until 5 days after fever resolution.

items.title: Chronic Carriage and Treatment Strategies
items.content: Chronic carriers of S. Typhi, particularly those with fluoroquinolone-susceptible strains, can be treated effectively with specific antibiotics, while resistant cases may require alternative strategies.
keyPoints: Approximately 2–5% of patients become chronic carriers of fluoroquinolone-susceptible S. Typhi.
Treatment for 4 weeks with oral ciprofloxacin or similar agents results in an ~80% eradication rate.
Azithromycin can be used for fluoroquinolone-resistant strains, though clinical experience is limited.
Oral amoxicillin may be considered if the strain is susceptible to ampicillin but has lower eradication rates than fluoroquinolones.

items.title: Vaccination Recommendations for Typhoid Fever in Travelers
items.content: Travelers to regions with a high risk of S. Typhi exposure should consider vaccination, especially when visiting southern Asia and other developing areas.
keyPoints: Two typhoid vaccines available in the U.S.: Ty21a (oral) and Vi CPS (parenteral).
Ty21a requires four doses with revaccination every 5 years; Vi CPS is a single dose with a booster every 2 years.
Vaccines are moderately effective (80%) for U.S. travelers.
Recommended for travel to areas with moderate to high risk of S. Typhi, including southern Asia and other developing regions.

items.title: Efficacy and Recommendations for Typhoid Conjugate Vaccines (TCV)
items.content: Typhoid conjugate vaccines are prioritized for use in high-incidence countries due to their effectiveness in young children and long-lasting immunity.
keyPoints: Two TCVs recommended by WHO: Typarb TCV and TYPHIBEV.
Single intramuscular dose is 79–95% effective, with protection up to 7 years.
Routinely introduced in national immunization programs in countries like Pakistan, Nepal, Liberia, Zimbabwe, Malawi, and Samoa.

items.title: Role of Vaccination in Enteric Fever Prevention and Public Health Management
items.content: Vaccination is an adjunct to preventive measures against enteric fever; it does not replace the need for avoiding high-risk foods.
keyPoints: Enteric fever is a notifiable disease in the U.S., aiding public health tracking and management.
1–4% of S. Typhi-infected patients become chronic carriers, necessitating monitoring and treatment.
Vaccination is recommended for those at occupational risk or household contacts of known carriers.

items.title: Global Impact and Incidence of NTS Infections
items.content: Non-typhoidal Salmonella (NTS) infections are a significant global health issue, causing approximately 93–150 million enteric infections and 60,000–155,000 deaths annually worldwide. In the United States, NTS results in about 1.35 million illnesses, 26,500 hospitalizations, and 420 deaths each year.
keyPoints: Worldwide: ~93–150 million enteric infections, ~60,000–155,000 deaths annually
United States: ~1.35 million illnesses, 26,500 hospitalizations, 420 deaths per year
Incidence in the U.S.: 14.5 cases per 100,000 persons (second highest after Campylobacter)

items.title: Seasonal and Demographic Patterns of NTS Infections
items.content: The incidence of NTS infections peaks during the rainy season in tropical climates and warmer months in temperate regions, aligning with food-borne outbreaks. The highest morbidity and mortality rates are observed among the elderly, infants, and immunocompromised individuals.
keyPoints: Seasonal peak: Rainy season in tropics, warmer months in temperate climates
High-risk groups: Elderly, infants, immunocompromised (e.g., hemoglobinopathies, HIV)

items.title: Emergence and Spread of Invasive NTS Strains
items.content: Invasive NTS strains, particularly S. Typhimurium ST313 and S. Enteritidis ST11, have emerged in sub-Saharan Africa and spread to other regions like South Asia, the UK, and Brazil. These strains are often multi-drug resistant (MDR) and adapted for person-to-person transmission.
keyPoints: Invasive NTS: ~535,000 cases, ~77,500 deaths globally in 2017
High incidence in children <5 years old in West Africa (>100 per 100,000 person-years)
MDR strains: Resistance to ampicillin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, chloramphenicol; some resistant to ceftriaxone or ciprofloxacin

items.title: Centralization of Food Processing Increases NTS Incidence
items.content: The centralization of food processing and widespread distribution contribute to the increased incidence of non-typhoidal Salmonella (NTS) in developed countries. This is due to the potential for a single contamination event at a production site to affect large quantities of distributed products.
keyPoints: Centralized food processing increases NTS incidence in developed countries.
Manufactured foods linked to outbreaks include peanut butter, milk products, and various processed foods.
Fresh produce such as alfalfa sprouts and cantaloupe can also be sources when contaminated by manure or water.

items.title: NTS Infection from Exotic Pets
items.content: Exotic pets, particularly reptiles and amphibians, are significant sources of NTS infections. These infections often affect young children more severely, leading to hospitalization and prolonged outbreaks.
keyPoints: Reptiles and amphibians are common sources of NTS in the U.S.
NTS from animal contact affects young children (<1–4 years) more commonly.
Outbreaks linked to pets result in higher rates of hospitalization.

items.title: Antibiotic Resistance Trends in NTS
items.content: NTS species exhibit increasing antibiotic resistance, with significant implications for treatment and public health. This resistance is linked to the use of antimicrobial agents in food animals.
keyPoints: Clinically important resistant NTS infections increased by 40% from 2015–2016 compared to 2004–2008.
Invasive NTS infections in sub-Saharan Africa show high multidrug resistance (75%).
3.0% of U.S. human NTS isolates were ceftriaxone resistant in 2021.

items.title: Gastroenteritis: Self-limited or Risk for Dehydration and Invasive Disease?
items.content: NTS gastroenteritis typically resolves within a few days, but certain populations are at higher risk of complications. Antibiotics are generally not recommended unless specific high-risk groups are involved.
keyPoints: Diarrhea usually resolves in 3–7 days; fever within 72 hours.

Antibiotic treatment is not typically recommended for acute NTS gastroenteritis but may be necessary for neonates, the elderly, and immunosuppressed patients due to risk of dehydration and invasive infection.
Acute NTS gastroenteritis can increase the risk of dyspepsia and irritable bowel syndrome by threefold at 1 year.

items.title: Bacteremia and Endovascular Infections: When to Suspect and Treat?
items.content: A small percentage of NTS gastroenteritis cases progress to bacteremia, with a subset developing severe endovascular infections. High-risk groups include infants, the elderly, and immunocompromised individuals.
keyPoints: Up to 8% of patients with NTS gastroenteritis develop bacteremia; 5–10% of these may develop localized infections.
Endovascular infection should be suspected in cases of high-grade or persistent bacteremia, especially if there are preexisting conditions like valvular heart disease.
NTS is a common cause of community-acquired bloodstream infection in children and HIV-infected adults in sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia.

items.title: Central Nervous System Infections: High Risk and Severe Outcomes?
items.content: NTS meningitis is most severe in infants and HIV-infected adults, with high mortality rates. Other CNS infections are rare but can lead to serious complications.
keyPoints: NTS meningitis occurs primarily in infants 1–4 months old and adults with HIV infection.
Severe sequelae of NTS meningitis include seizures, hydrocephalus, brain infarction, and mental retardation, with up to 60% mortality rate.
Other CNS infections like ventriculitis, subdural empyema, and brain abscesses are rare but serious.

items.title: When to Obtain Blood Cultures and Imaging for NTS Infection
items.content: Blood cultures are essential in patients with prolonged or recurrent fever, particularly when high-grade bacteremia is suspected (>50% of three or more sets positive). Imaging techniques such as echocardiography, CT, and indium-labeled white cell scanning should be utilized to identify localized infections. Cultures from joint fluid, bone biopsies, abscess drainage, or cerebrospinal fluid are indicated when a specific site of infection is suspected.
keyPoints: Blood cultures warranted for prolonged/recurrent fever
>50% positive blood culture sets indicate high-grade bacteremia
Use echocardiography, CT, and indium-labeled white cell scanning for localized infections
Culture joint fluid, bone biopsy, abscess drainage, or CSF when infection is suspected at specific sites

items.title: Antibiotic Management for NTS Infections
items.content: Routine antibiotic treatment is not recommended for uncomplicated NTS gastroenteritis due to lack of benefit and potential adverse effects. However, preemptive antibiotics should be considered for high-risk patients (e.g., neonates up to 3 months, >50 years with atherosclerosis, immunosuppressed individuals). Empirical therapy for severe cases includes third-generation cephalosporins or fluoroquinolones. Treatment duration varies: 48–72 hours until afebrile for most, but 7–14 days for low-grade bacteremia and longer for HIV/AIDS patients. Chronic carriers may require prolonged antibiotic courses.
keyPoints: Avoid antibiotics for uncomplicated NTS gastroenteritis
Consider preemptive antibiotics for high-risk groups (e.g., neonates, >50 years with atherosclerosis)
Empirical therapy: third-generation cephalosporin or fluoroquinolone for severe cases
48–72 hours of antibiotic treatment until afebrile; 7–14 days for low-grade bacteremia
HIV/AIDS patients need 1–2 weeks IV then 4 weeks oral therapy with fluoroquinolones
Chronic carriers require prolonged antibiotics

items.title: Importance of Rapid Diagnostics for Invasive NTS
items.content: Rapid, point-of-care diagnostics are essential to reduce morbidity and mortality associated with invasive non-typhoidal Salmonella (NTS) infections.
keyPoints: No available vaccine against invasive NTS disease.
Candidate vaccines are O-antigen based with S. Typhimurium and S. Enteritidis components.
Rapid diagnostics needed to reduce morbidity and mortality.

items.title: Strategies for Reducing NTS Incidence in Food Supply Chain
items.content: Effective risk-reduction strategies require monitoring every step of the food supply chain, from farm sources to finished foods, and prudent use of antimicrobials.
keyPoints: Modest declines in NTS incidence despite prevention efforts.
Monitoring needed at all steps: farm, slaughter, processing, storage, transport, preparation.
Contaminated food can be made safe by pasteurization, irradiation, or cooking.
Report all NTS cases to public health for outbreak tracking and source identification.
Prudent antimicrobial use in humans and animals limits MDR and XDR Salmonella emergence.

items.question: Which subspecies of Salmonella enterica includes almost all serotypes pathogenic for humans?
items.explanation: S. enterica subspecies I contains almost all the serotypes pathogenic for humans.

items.question: Which antigen is restricted to S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi C?
items.explanation: The Vi antigen is restricted to S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi C.

items.question: Which characteristic is NOT associated with Salmonellae, except for S. Gallinarum-Pullorum?
options: Production of gas on sugar fermentation
items.explanation: All salmonellae except S. Gallinarum-Pullorum are motile and produce gas; only 1% ferment lactose.

items.question: Which factors increase susceptibility to Salmonella infection?
options: High stomach acidity and intact intestinal integrity
Decreased stomach acidity or compromised intestinal integrity
Increased stomach acidity and enhanced intestinal integrity
Normal stomach acidity and normal intestinal microbiome
items.explanation: Conditions that decrease either stomach acidity or intestinal integrity increase susceptibility to Salmonella infection.

items.question: How do S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi penetrate the intestinal layer?
options: Through passive diffusion across epithelial cells
By triggering membrane ruffles in normally nonphagocytic epithelial cells
By direct invasion of enterocytes without any cellular changes
By utilizing flagella to move through the mucus layer
items.explanation: Salmonellae can trigger the formation of membrane ruffles in normally nonphagocytic epithelial cells, allowing penetration.

items.question: What role does the PhoP/PhoQ regulatory system play in Salmonella survival?
options: It decreases bacterial protein synthesis to avoid detection.
It alters the outer membrane to resist antimicrobial activities.
It promotes the release of toxins outside host cells.
It inhibits the type III secretion system.
items.explanation: PhoP/PhoQ triggers alteration of the outer membrane, increasing resistance to microbial activities.

items.question: What characterizes NTS gastroenteritis compared to enteric fever?
options: Infiltration of mononuclear cells into small-bowel mucosa
Massive polymorphonuclear leukocyte infiltration in the bowel mucosa
Absence of inflammatory response in the intestinal tract
Effective colonization of the intestinal tract by typhoidal Salmonellae
items.explanation: NTS gastroenteritis is characterized by massive polymorphonuclear leukocyte infiltration into both large- and small-bowel mucosa.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a known transmission route for enteric fever?
options: Fecal contamination by ill or asymptomatic chronic carriers
Health care workers acquiring it from exposure to infected patients
items.explanation: Enteric fever is transmitted only through humans, not animals.

items.question: Which region has the highest estimated annual incidence rates of typhoid fever?
items.explanation: The Indian subcontinent, including Eastern Mediterranean and African regions, has the highest incidence rates.

items.question: What is a common risk factor for enteric fever in endemic regions?
options: Fecally contaminated drinking water or ice
items.explanation: Fecally contaminated drinking water or ice is a common risk factor.

items.question: Which antibiotic resistance emerged in S. Typhi strains on the Indian subcontinent due to increased use of fluoroquinolones?
options: Decreased susceptibility or resistance to ciprofloxacin
items.explanation: Increased use of fluoroquinolones led to decreased susceptibility or resistance to ciprofloxacin.

items.question: Which symptom is reported by approximately 80% of patients with enteric fever on initial medical evaluation?
items.explanation: Headache is reported in 80% of cases.

items.question: What percentage of patients with enteric fever develop a rash known as 'rose spots'?
items.explanation: ~30% of patients develop rose spots.

items.question: During which weeks are gastrointestinal bleeding and intestinal perforation most likely to occur in typhoid fever?
items.explanation: These complications commonly occur in the third and fourth weeks.

items.question: Which of the following is a neurologic manifestation that can occur in patients with typhoid fever?
items.explanation: Neurologic manifestations include meningitis.

items.question: Which diagnostic method has a sensitivity of approximately 80% for detecting S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi?
items.explanation: Bone marrow culture is ~80% sensitive for detecting S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi.

items.question: What is the sensitivity range of rapid immunodiagnostic tests like Tubex and Typhidot in diagnosing typhoid fever?
items.explanation: Rapid immunodiagnostic tests have sensitivities ranging from ~70% to 80%.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is not reduced in yield by up to 5 days of prior antibiotic therapy?
items.explanation: The yield of bone marrow culture is not reduced by up to 5 days of prior antibiotic therapy.

items.question: What is the recommended empirical treatment for enteric fever in patients with a history of travel to Pakistan due to concerns about ceftriaxone-resistant S. Typhi?
items.explanation: Patients with concern for ceftriaxone-resistant S. Typhi infection based on a history of travel to Pakistan should be treated empirically with a carbapenem or azithromycin.

items.question: Which antibiotic is associated with lower rates of treatment failure and shorter durations of hospitalization against DSC strains compared to fluoroquinolones?
items.explanation: Against DSC strains, azithromycin is associated with lower rates of treatment failure and shorter durations of hospitalization than are fluoroquinolones.

items.question: What is the mortality rate for untreated enteric fever?
items.explanation: Mortality rates rise to 10–30% if untreated.

items.question: Which antibiotic is not recommended for empirical treatment of enteric fever in regions with high prevalence of ciprofloxacin-resistant strains?
items.explanation: Due to the high prevalence of ciprofloxacin-resistant strains, fluoroquinolones should no longer be used for empirical treatment in these regions.

items.question: Which typhoid vaccine requires a full four-dose series with revaccination every 5 years?
items.explanation: The Ty21a oral live attenuated S. Typhi vaccine requires a full four-dose series with revaccination every 5 years.

items.question: What is the minimal age for vaccination with the Vi CPS typhoid vaccine?
items.explanation: The minimal age for vaccination with the Vi CPS typhoid vaccine is 2 years.

items.question: Which statement best describes the efficacy of Ty21a and Vi CPS vaccines in endemic areas according to a 2018 meta-analysis?
options: Ty21a: 55% at 2.5-3 years, Vi CPS: 50% at 3 years
Ty21a: 50% at 2.5-3 years, Vi CPS: 55% at 3 years
items.explanation: The cumulative efficacy was 50% for Ty21a at 2.5 to 3 years and 55% for Vi CPS at 3 years.

items.question: Which typhoid conjugate vaccines (TCVs) have been prioritized by the World Health Organization?
items.explanation: The World Health Organization has prioritized Typarb TCV and TYPHIBEV to prevent typhoid fever in high-incidence countries.

items.question: Which Salmonella serotype is primarily associated with pigs and pork products in Europe?
items.explanation: Salmonella serotype 4,[5],12:i:- is primarily associated with pigs and pork products in Europe.

items.question: What is the second most common cause of food-borne outbreaks after norovirus in the United States?
items.explanation: NTS are the second most common cause of food-borne outbreaks after norovirus in the United States.

items.question: Which group is at highest risk for morbidity and mortality associated with NTS?
items.explanation: The highest rates of morbidity and mortality from NTS are among the elderly, infants, and immunocompromised individuals.

items.question: Which Salmonella serotype has emerged as a major cause of food-borne disease associated with chicken eggs since the 1980s?
items.explanation: S. Enteritidis has emerged as a major cause of food-borne disease associated with chicken eggs since the 1980s.

items.question: Which manufactured foods have recent multistate Salmonella outbreaks been traced to?
options: Peanut butter, milk products including powdered infant formula, and various processed foods
Only peanut butter and milk products
Fresh produce such as alfalfa sprouts and cantaloupe
items.explanation: Recent multistate Salmonella outbreaks have been traced to manufactured foods including peanut butter, milk products (including powdered infant formula), and various processed foods.

items.question: Which group is more commonly affected by NTS linked to animal contact?
options: Elderly individuals over the age of 65
items.explanation: Outbreaks of NTS linked to animal contact more commonly affect young children (<1–4 years of age).

items.question: What is a common source of contamination for fresh produce leading to Salmonella outbreaks?
options: Manure or water at a single site
items.explanation: Fresh produce becomes contaminated by manure or water at a single site and then is widely distributed.

items.question: What has contributed to the increased incidence of NTS in developed countries?
options: Centralization of food processing and widespread food distribution
Decreased hygiene standards in food handling
items.explanation: Centralization of food processing and widespread food distribution have contributed to the increased incidence of NTS.

items.question: Which clinical feature is most commonly associated with acute NTS gastroenteritis?
items.explanation: Diarrheal stools in NTS gastroenteritis are usually loose and nonbloody.

items.question: Which patient population is especially susceptible to dehydration and invasive infection from NTS gastroenteritis?
options: Neonates, the elderly, and immunosuppressed patients
items.explanation: Neonates, the elderly, and immunosuppressed patients are especially susceptible to dehydration and invasive infection from NTS gastroenteritis.

items.question: What is a potential long-term complication associated with acute NTS gastroenteritis?
options: Increased risk of dyspepsia and irritable bowel syndrome
items.explanation: Acute NTS gastroenteritis is associated with a threefold increased risk of dyspepsia and irritable bowel syndrome at 1 year.

items.question: Which strain of Salmonella is most commonly linked to invasive disease in sub-Saharan Africa?
items.explanation: S. Typhimurium ST 131 is the most common cause of invasive NTS disease in sub-Saharan Africa.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is increasingly used for identifying Salmonella due to its increased sensitivity and rapid turnaround?
items.explanation: Culture-independent molecular diagnostic tests are increasingly used due to increased sensitivity and rapid turnaround.

items.question: When should blood cultures be obtained in patients with suspected NTS infection?
options: Whenever a patient has prolonged or recurrent fever
Only if the patient is immunocompromised
If there are no symptoms present
items.explanation: Blood cultures should be obtained whenever a patient has prolonged or recurrent fever.

items.question: Which imaging modality is used to identify localized infection in patients with suspected endovascular infection?
items.explanation: Echocardiography, CT, and indium-labeled white cell scanning are used to identify localized infection.

items.question: Which groups are targeted for protection by candidate human NTS vaccines?
options: Young children, persons with HIV infection, those with malaria and sickle cell disease
Only young children and the elderly
Persons with diabetes and cardiovascular diseases
items.explanation: Candidate human NTS vaccines target young children and at-risk groups including persons with HIV infection, malaria, and sickle cell disease.

items.question: What is critically needed to reduce the morbidity and mortality associated with invasive NTS infection?
options: Increased use of antibiotics in food animals
items.explanation: Rapid, point-of-care diagnostics are critically needed to reduce the morbidity and mortality associated with invasive NTS infection.

items.question: What methods can make contaminated food safe for consumption?
items.explanation: Contaminated food can be made safe for consumption by pasteurization, irradiation, or proper cooking.

items.statement: Serotyping of Salmonella is based on antigenically diverse surface structures including the O, Vi, and H antigens.
items.explanation: Serotyping involves the O antigen (lipopolysaccharide), Vi antigen (restricted to S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi C), and H flagellar antigen.

items.statement: All Salmonella serotypes ferment lactose.
items.explanation: Only 1% of clinical isolates of Salmonella ferment lactose, making it a rare characteristic.

items.statement: Typhoidal Salmonellae effectively colonize the intestinal tract.
items.explanation: Typhoidal Salmonellae do not effectively colonize the intestinal tract but have evolved as systemic pathogens.

items.statement: Salmonella infections begin with ingestion of organisms in contaminated food or water.
items.explanation: All Salmonella infections start with the ingestion of organisms, most commonly from contaminated sources.

items.statement: Enteric fever is more common in rural areas than urban poor neighborhoods.
items.explanation: Enteric fever is more common in poor neighborhoods of large cities.

items.statement: Multidrug-resistant strains of S. Typhi emerged in the 1980s.
items.explanation: MDR strains of S. Typhi did emerge in the 1980s.

items.statement: S. Paratyphi A causes more severe disease than S. Typhi.
items.explanation: S. Paratyphi A is thought to cause milder disease than S. Typhi.

items.statement: Chronic carriage of S. Typhi can lead to an increased risk of gallbladder cancer.
items.explanation: Chronic carriage is associated with an increased risk of gallbladder cancer.

items.statement: A positive blood culture alone is sufficient for the definitive diagnosis of enteric fever.
items.explanation: Definitive diagnosis requires isolation from multiple sources, not just a positive blood culture.

items.statement: PCR detection of S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi in the blood has consistent sensitivity across all gene targets.
items.explanation: The sensitivity of PCR detection varies depending on the gene targets.

items.statement: Oral azithromycin is recommended for the treatment of uncomplicated enteric fever, including ESBL, DSC, and fluoroquinolone-resistant strains.
items.explanation: Oral azithromycin is indeed recommended for treating these types of enteric fever.

items.statement: Most patients with uncomplicated enteric fever require hospitalization.
items.explanation: Most patients with uncomplicated enteric fever can be managed at home with oral antibiotics and antipyretics.

items.statement: Typhoid vaccine is required for international travel.
items.explanation: Typhoid vaccine is not required but recommended for travelers to areas with moderate to high risk of S. Typhi exposure.

items.statement: A single dose of TCV can provide protection lasting up to 7 years.
items.explanation: A single intramuscular 0.5-mL dose of either TCV is safe and provides antibody response persisting up to 7 years.

items.statement: The incidence rate of NTS infection in the United States was below the U.S. Healthy People 2030 goal of 11.5 cases per 100,000 persons in 2022.
items.explanation: In 2022, the incidence rate was above the U.S. Healthy People 2030 goal.

items.statement: S. Typhimurium and S. Enteritidis are the most common serotypes causing human disease globally.
items.explanation: Globally, S. Typhimurium and S. Enteritidis are indeed the most common serotypes causing human disease.

items.statement: The majority of clinically important resistant NTS infections in the United States are associated with outbreaks.
items.explanation: Infections caused by NTS with any antimicrobial resistance compared to NTS with no resistance are less likely to be associated with an outbreak.

items.statement: Ceftriaxone-resistant NTS strains are more common in invasive isolates from sub-Saharan Africa than in the United States.
items.explanation: Ceftriaxone resistance is more common among invasive NTS isolates from sub-Saharan Africa (>5%) compared to 3.0% in the United States.

items.statement: NTS bacteremia is commonly associated with diarrhea.
items.explanation: NTS bacteremia among children is not typically associated with diarrhea.

items.statement: Antibiotic treatment is usually recommended for acute NTS gastroenteritis.
items.explanation: Antibiotic treatment is usually not recommended for acute NTS gastroenteritis as it may prolong fecal carriage.

items.statement: Antibiotics should be routinely used to treat uncomplicated NTS gastroenteritis.
items.explanation: Antibiotics are not recommended for routine treatment of uncomplicated NTS gastroenteritis.

items.statement: Patients with HIV/AIDS and NTS bacteremia should receive 1–2 weeks of IV antibiotic therapy followed by 4 weeks of oral therapy.
items.explanation: This is the recommended treatment regimen for patients with HIV/AIDS and NTS bacteremia.

items.statement: There is an available vaccine to protect against invasive NTS disease.
items.explanation: There is no available vaccine to protect against invasive NTS disease.

items.statement: All cases of NTS infection should be reported to local public health departments.
items.explanation: Reporting all cases helps identify sources and anticipate outbreaks.

items.assertion: Salmonellae use a type III secretion system to alter host cell signaling.
items.reason: This system delivers bacterial proteins into the cytoplasm of epithelial cells, altering the actin cytoskeleton.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: NTS gastroenteritis symptoms result from mononuclear cell infiltration.
items.reason: This response is due to interleukin 8 secretion by intestinal cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion; instead, it explains neutrophil infiltration.

items.assertion: Enteric fever has become rare in developed nations.
items.reason: Improvements in food handling and water/sewage treatment have reduced the incidence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Most cases of enteric fever in the United States are associated with international travel.
items.reason: The majority of travelers diagnosed with typhoid fever have received S. Typhi vaccine within the previous 5 years.
items.explanation: While most cases are associated with travel, few travelers had been vaccinated.

items.assertion: Rose spots are a common early physical finding in enteric fever.
items.reason: Rose spots make up a faint, salmon-colored rash that is evident in ~30% of patients at the end of the first week.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Intestinal perforation in typhoid fever is due to hyperplasia of Peyer's patches.
items.reason: Intestinal perforation results from ulceration and necrosis of the ileocecal Peyer's patches at the initial site of Salmonella infiltration.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason incorrectly attributes the cause to hyperplasia instead of ulceration and necrosis.

items.assertion: Steroid treatment beyond 48 hours may increase the relapse rate in enteric fever.
items.reason: Severe enteric fever remains one of the few indications for glucocorticoid treatment of an acute bacterial infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason does not explain why steroid treatment beyond 48 hours may increase the relapse rate.

items.assertion: A 4-week course of oral azithromycin can be used to treat carriers with fluoroquinolone-resistant strains.
items.reason: Clinical experience is limited for using azithromycin in treating these carriers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not negate the potential use of azithromycin.

items.assertion: Unconjugated typhoid vaccines are poorly immunogenic in children <5 years of age.
items.reason: This is due to their limited ability to elicit T cell-dependent immune responses and immunologic memory.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why unconjugated vaccines are poorly immunogenic in young children.

items.assertion: Immunization is a substitute for avoiding high-risk foods and beverages.
items.reason: The protective efficacy of vaccine can be overcome by the high inocula commonly encountered in food-borne exposures.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because immunization is an adjunct, not a substitute for avoiding high-risk foods and beverages.

items.assertion: The incidence of invasive NTS infection is highest in children under 5 years of age.
items.reason: This high incidence is associated with malaria, HIV, malnutrition, and unclean drinking water sources.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the incidence is highest in children under 5.

items.assertion: S. Enteritidis infection of hens results in contamination of egg contents after shell deposition.
items.reason: Infection spreads to egg-laying hens from breeding flocks and through contact with rodents and manure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the contamination occurs before shell deposition, but the reason is true.

items.assertion: Increasing antibiotic resistance in NTS species is a global problem.
items.reason: This has been linked to the widespread use of antimicrobial agents in food animals and especially in animal feed.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Infections caused by NTS with antimicrobial resistance are more likely to be associated with international travel.
items.reason: These infections have a higher risk of hospitalization, longer hospital stays, and increased mortality.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why resistant NTS infections are more likely to be associated with international travel.

items.assertion: NTS meningitis often results in severe sequelae.
items.reason: It most commonly develops in infants and adults with HIV infection, leading to complications like seizures and brain infarction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why NTS meningitis often results in severe sequelae.

items.assertion: Localized infections due to NTS are common.
items.reason: They usually manifest as hepatic or splenic abscesses, especially in patients with sickle cell disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because localized infections due to NTS are rare; however, the reason correctly identifies a risk factor for such infections.

items.assertion: The incidence of NTS in the United States has declined significantly compared to other food-borne pathogens.
items.reason: Efforts have been made to prevent or reduce bacterial contamination of animal-derived food products and improve food-safety education.
items.explanation: The decline in NTS incidence has been modest, not significant, despite efforts.
